conversation.content: My order hasn't arrived and it's been 2 weeks. The tracking says it was delivered but I never received it.

candidate_answer: I'm sorry to hear about this situation. A package showing as delivered but not received is concerning. Here's what I recommend: First, check with neighbors or your building's front desk in case it was left there. Second, check any safe spots where delivery drivers sometimes leave packages. If you still can't locate it, I'll file a missing package investigation with the carrier and arrange either a replacement shipment or a full refund — whichever you prefer. This typically takes 2-3 business days to resolve.
rationale: Excellent empathy and clear resolution steps.

conversation.content: Is your product compatible with Mac?
Yes! Our product is fully compatible with macOS. We support macOS 12 (Monterey) and later.
Great, what about the M1 chip specifically? I heard some apps have issues with it.

candidate_answer: Good question! Our product runs natively on Apple Silicon (M1, M2, M3 chips) — no Rosetta translation needed. We released our universal binary in version 3.2, so make sure you're running that version or later. Performance on M1 is actually about 40% better than on Intel Macs due to the ARM optimization.
rationale: Directly answers the specific chip question.

conversation.content: Is your product compatible with Mac?
Yes! Our product is fully compatible with macOS. We support macOS 12 (Monterey) and later.

candidate_answer: Yes it works on Mac as I just mentioned.

conversation.content: Is your product compatible with Mac?
Yes! Our product is fully compatible with macOS. We support macOS 12 (Monterey) and later.
Great, what about the M1 chip specifically? I heard some apps have issues with it.

candidate_answer: The M1 chip uses a RISC-based ARM architecture that implements a heterogeneous computing paradigm with performance and efficiency cores. The instruction set architecture differs fundamentally from the x86-64 ISA used in Intel processors, necessitating either native ARM64 compilation or binary translation via the Rosetta 2 dynamic recompiler. Our application's Mach-O binary is compiled as a universal binary containing both x86_64 and arm64 slices.
rationale: Overly technical and dense for a user.

conversation.content: I accidentally deleted important files from my cloud storage. Can they be recovered?

candidate_answer: Don't worry — deleted files go to our Trash folder and are recoverable for 30 days. Go to Cloud Storage > Trash, select the files you need, and click Restore. They'll return to their original location. If it's been more than 30 days, our support team can attempt a recovery from backups, but this isn't guaranteed. Would you like me to check how long ago the files were deleted?

conversation.content: I accidentally deleted important files from my cloud storage. Can they be recovered?

candidate_answer: Unfortunately once files are deleted they are permanently gone and cannot be recovered under any circumstances. You should have made backups. This is why we always recommend keeping local copies of important documents.
rationale: Unhelpful, blames user, likely incorrect policy.

conversation.content: I'm getting an error message that says 'Error 503: Service Unavailable' when I try to log in.

candidate_answer: A 503 error means our servers are temporarily overloaded or undergoing maintenance. I've checked our status page and we're currently experiencing higher than normal traffic. The engineering team is working on scaling up capacity. Based on similar past incidents, this should be resolved within 30-60 minutes. You can check our real-time status at status.example.com. I apologize for the inconvenience.
rationale: Explains issue simply and provides status link.

conversation.content: I'm getting an error message that says 'Error 503: Service Unavailable' when I try to log in.

candidate_answer: Error 503 is an HTTP status code that indicates the server is not ready to handle the request. Common causes include server overload, maintenance windows, or upstream dependency failures. The HTTP specification defines 503 as a temporary condition which should be alleviated after some delay. The server MAY send a Retry-After header field to suggest an appropriate amount of time for the client to wait before retrying the request, as specified in RFC 7231 Section 7.1.3.
rationale: Too academic and technical for standard user.
